intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard works best with warm, inviting aesthetics that feel like a caring companion rather than a clinical tool
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward human-centered, emotionally resonant interfaces; likely values comfort and trust over precision aesthetics
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cooler, more clinical or neutral palettes; may value clarity and objectivity over warmth; dashboard should feel scientific rather than intimate

evidence.hypothesis: A health dashboard works best with warm, inviting aesthetics that feel like a caring companion rather than a clinical tool
evidence.implication: User gravitates toward human-centered, emotionally resonant interfaces; likely values comfort and trust over precision aesthetics

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer gentle, approachable UI elements over rigid or angular designs
facade.axisTargeted: Roundness vs sharpness in component shapes
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into curved corners, smooth buttons, and organic card shapes for a less clinical feel
facade.rejectImplies: Sharp corners, grid-aligned edges, and geometric precision might feel more appropriate for data-heavy dashboards

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, conversational tone (think text-to-a-friend) will feel more motivating for daily health logging than formal clinical language
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and personality: friendly vs professional
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers warm, approachable language; likely to engage with casual UI copy, conversational prompts, and informal progress messages
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clinical precision and formal tone; will respond better to medical/scientific language, structured terminology, and neutral UI voice

facade.hypothesis: A personal health dashboard works better with compact weekly summaries rather than scrolling through individual data points
facade.axisTargeted: Granularity & temporal aggregation — do users want zoomed-out summaries or granular daily logs?
facade.label: Weekly snapshot cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick scanning over drill-down; favors card-based layouts with pre-digested weekly insights; wants to see patterns at a glance.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants daily-level detail readily visible; prefers scrollable lists or timelines; needs to see individual data points without extra clicks or aggregation.

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar for experiment selection paired with spacious card-based content will feel less cluttered and support long-term scanning of many experiments
facade.axisTargeted: layout structure and density trade-off (sidebar vs tabs; cards vs lists; spacious vs dense)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User wants visual separation between navigation and content; prioritizes overview scanning over linear flow; can handle white space
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers integrated navigation (tabs/inline) or denser layouts; wants more content density; sidebars feel like wasted horizontal space

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer animated data entry and visualization transitions over instant chart updates, reducing cognitive load when tracking experiments.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — animated transitions vs instant rendering
facade.label: Smooth slide-in charts
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use fluid, eased animations when data points appear, metrics update, and views shift. Transitions create a sense of flow and narrative to the data.
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should render new charts, metrics, and sections instantly without animation. Speed and clarity matter more than motion. Users scan data, not watch it arrive.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard needs sparse, distraction-free visualizations that let data breathe rather than overwhelming users with information density
facade.axisTargeted: Organic vs Digital — whether the interface feels handcrafted and natural or optimized and information-dense
facade.label: Minimal data displays
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers clarity and restraint; likely values insight over completeness; may find busy dashboards anxiety-inducing
facade.rejectImplies: User wants comprehensive views and rich context; density signals thoroughness and value; prefers maximum information availability at a glance
